straight_beat.name: Reuben Tachibana
straight_beat.column:
The Athletics didn’t need a walk-off home run or a dramatic ninth-inning rally. They needed a pitcher who could navigate a game without giving up a run, and they got it. Frankie Montas, the lefty who’s been a quiet pillar of this rotation, did just that. He allowed one hit, struck out six, and didn’t walk a soul. The Mets, for all their talent, couldn’t string together a single base hit against him. It was the kind of performance that makes you wonder why the A’s aren’t winning more games.  

The ledger says otherwise.  

You can’t ignore the numbers: 8-7, -1 in the AL West. But here’s the thing about this team. They’re built to grind out wins, not blow them out. Montas’s gem was a

optimist.name: Penny Halloran
optimist.column:
The Athletics lost yesterday, 1-0 to the Mets, and the record now reads 8-7, with a -1.5 game lead in the AL West. But here’s the thing: this team isn’t built to fold. Not yet. Not when the calendar pages are still turning, and the ghosts of October baseball still linger in the ballpark like a stubborn scent. You look at Soderstrom’s cut and tell me we’re not two years from playing October baseball, wherever that field happens to be.  

Let’s not mince words—this was a stinker. A one-run loss, a bullpen that couldn’t hold a candle to the Mets’ fire, and a lineup that flattered the ball more than it chased it. But here’s the rub: the Athletics are still in this race. They’re not the team that’s been left behind. They

pessimist.name: Gil Mosbacher
pessimist.column:
The A’s won yesterday. That’s the first thing to note. A 1-0 decision over the Mets, a team that’s been a dumpster fire since 2015. So, yes, the A’s took a step forward. But let’s not confuse a single game with a season. The team is 8-7, four games behind the AL West, and the only thing that’s moving is the needle on the scoreboard in the wrong direction.  

The pitching staff, for all its struggles, managed to keep the Mets’ offense in check. That’s a small victory, but it’s the kind of thing that feels like a mirage in a desert of mediocrity. The A’s have been a team that’s never been good at scoring runs, and yesterday was no exception. One run. One game. One more reason to wonder how this team keeps finding ways to disappoint.  

The front office, of course, has its own brand of misery. John Fisher’s latest moves—trades, free agent signings, roster tweaks—have become a litany of missed opportunities. You can look at the spreadsheet I keep, the one filled with names of players Fisher didn’t pay to bring in, and it’s a testament to a front office that’s either out of touch or actively sabotaging itself. The A’s have a history of trading away potential, and Fisher’s smile during these transactions is the kind of thing that makes you want to throw a tantrum in the stands.  

But let’s not forget the bullpen. That’s the real poison. A team can’t win games if its closer can’t close them, and the A’s have been a circus in the seventh inning. The offense isn’t scoring, the pitching isn’t dominating, and the bullpen is a ticking time bomb. It’s the kind of thing that makes you think the 2
